coredata.dc:title: Hydrogel-based 3D evaporator with cross-linked fixation by carbon dots for ultra-high and stable solar steam generation

coredata.dc:description: Solar-driven water steam generation had been recognized as a promising strategy to address water scarcity in an environmentally friendly and cost-effective manner, and the development of an evaporator capable of efficient evaporation and stable long-term operation remains a huge challenge in the field. Herein, a three-dimensional (3D) hydrogel-based evaporator consisting of sodium alginate (SA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and carbon dots (CDs) integrated into a melamine sponge (MS) structure was constructed, in which the CDs were firmly bonded to the SA/PVA double crosslinked network through hydrogen bonding, which improved the mechanical properties and load-bearing capacity of the evaporator. Crucially, as highly efficient light-absorbing carbon-based materials, the CDs were homogeneously dispersed in the hydrogel, significantly enhancing the photothermal conversion capability of the evaporator. The test results indicated that the evaporation rate of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator reached up to ultra-high solar steam generation rate of 4.79 kg m-2h−1 in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 1 sun irradiation, and exhibited the relatively excellent evaporation rate (4.13 kg m-2h−1) in high saline seawater (25 wt%). This study aimed to provide a simple and reliable modification method to enhance the evaporation performance and improve the hydrogel network of SA/PVA-based hydrogel evaporator and demonstrate their potential applications in seawater desalination and purification.

head.ce:title: Hydrogel-based 3D evaporator with cross-linked fixation by carbon dots for ultra-high and stable solar steam generation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar-driven water steam generation had been recognized as a promising strategy to address water scarcity in an environmentally friendly and cost-effective manner, and the development of an evaporator capable of efficient evaporation and stable long-term operation remains a huge challenge in the field. Herein, a three-dimensional (3D) hydrogel-based evaporator consisting of sodium alginate (SA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and carbon dots (CDs) integrated into a melamine sponge (MS) structure was constructed, in which the CDs were firmly bonded to the SA/PVA double crosslinked network through hydrogen bonding, which improved the mechanical properties and load-bearing capacity of the evaporator. Crucially, as highly efficient light-absorbing carbon-based materials, the CDs were homogeneously dispersed in the hydrogel, significantly enhancing the photothermal conversion capability of the evaporator. The test results indicated that the evaporation rate of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator reached up to ultra-high solar steam generation rate of 4.79 kg m-2h−1 in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 1 sun irradiation, and exhibited the relatively excellent evaporation rate (4.13 kg m-2h−1) in high saline seawater (25 wt%). This study aimed to provide a simple and reliable modification method to enhance the evaporation performance and improve the hydrogel network of SA/PVA-based hydrogel evaporator and demonstrate their potential applications in seawater desalination and purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-driven water steam generation has been recognized as a promising strategy, presenting a significant potential candidate for alleviating global water scarcity [1–5]. During this process, the structural stability and photothermal conversion efficiency of the evaporator play a decisive role, with the former ensuring that the evaporator is able to maintain efficient water transfer during continuous operation and the latter being able to significantly enhance the evaporation rate [6]. Thus, the development of high-performance solar evaporators with structural stability and excellent photothermal conversion efficiency is the key to promoting the development of solar desalination technology.

In recent years many materials have been employed for the construction of solar evaporators with high efficiency of photo-thermal conversion, stable water supply and efficient thermal management, such as carbon-based materials (carbon nanotubes [7], graphene [8]), polymer hydrogels (sodium alginate [9], chitosan [10], cellulose nanoparticles [11–15]), biomass materials [16,17], metal nanoparticles [18] and so forth. Notably, relying on its three-dimensional (3D) porous hydrophilic network structure [19], hydrogels with superior plasticity can provide a large number of microchannels to facilitate water transport and enhance the reflection and scattering of internal light, resulting in the capture of more light energy [20–22]. Among them, sodium alginate (SA), which is rich in numerous hydrophilic groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl), as a raw material for the synthesis of natural physical hydrogels, can be physically crosslinked under the action of Ca2+ to form hydrogels with a 3D porous hydrophilic network, possessing abundant micro-channels that can facilitate the transport of water [23]. Unfortunately, the hydrogel evaporators prepared with SA as the substrate have some drawbacks in terms of mechanical properties and structural stability, such as easy fracture, deformation, and degradation, which can lead to the loss of water evaporation function [24]. Hence, in order to ensure long-term stable operation, the modification and enhancement of SA-based hydrogel evaporators in terms of mechanical property are extremely important for its practical application.

Generally speaking, hybrid hydrogels are composed of two or more different types of natural or synthetic polymers, further combining the advantages of natural polymer-based hydrogels (good biocompatibility and safety) and synthetic polymer-based hydrogels (high mechanical strength) [25], thus solving the above problems. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a raw material for the synthesis of polymer-based hydrogels allows for the formation of unique dual-network hybrid hydrogels with SA through physical and chemical cross-linking, which significantly improves the mechanical property of the SA hydrogel, making it more resilient and stable [26]. Another aspect, as a sponge carrier material with a large pore network structure, melamine sponge (MS) has excellent water transport capability due to its pore network structure [27–29]. When SA/PVA hydrogels are applied on MS, nevertheless, unavoidable structural deformation occurs, resulting in deformation of the water transport channels, which reduces the water transport performance and leads to the precipitation of salt particles at the evaporation interface [30]. There is thereby an urgent need but it is still a significant challenge to select a material as a “rivet” to combine with SA/PVAS-MS double crosslinked network for improving the structural stability.

Carbon dots (CDs, less than 10 nm in diameter) with innocuity, excellent biocompatibility, and low-cost sources possess abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface, which can be utilized as a multifunctional component in the solar interface evaporator design [31–33]. Previous studies have reported that CDs exhibit excellent light-absorbing properties, thereby effectively enhancing the photothermal conversion efficiency of the evaporator when dispersed homogeneously in the evaporator [34]. Meanwhile, the surface hydrophilic groups of CDs can form various hydrogen bonds with water molecules to create weakly hydrogen-bonded intermediate water [35–38], which affects the overall energy requirements for steam generation [39–41]. Additionality, CDs with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl and so on [42,43]) can be connected to the double-crosslinked network of SA/PVA hydrogel through hydrogen bonding, which promotes effective cross-linking and polymerization, improving the mechanical property of the evaporator. In this regard, we attempt to combine CDs to cross-link with SA/PVA-MS hydrogel for constructing a highly effective and long-term stable solar steam generation system.

Based on the above inspiration, a 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS composite hydrogel solar evaporator was developed by cross-linking SA/PVA hydrogel network immobilized by CDs, which not only enhanced the mechanical properties and load-bearing capacity of the evaporator, but also significantly improved its photothermal conversion capacity. The evaporation rate of the evaporator is up to 4.79 kg m-2h−1 in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under 1 sunlight intensity. Moreover, based on the excellent resistance to salt deposition of the evaporator, the evaporation rate is still excellent (4.13 kg m-2h−1) in high saline seawater (25 wt%), which is well beyond that of comparable SA/PVA-based evaporators in recent reports. This work provides a simple and reliable modification method to optimize the evaporation performance of SA/PVA-based hydrogel evaporators, demonstrating its broad application prospects in the field of seawater desalination and purification.

Sodium alginate (C6H7NaO6, SA), polyvinyl alcohol ([C2H4O]n, PVA), calcium chloride (CaCl2), glutaraldehyde (C5H8O2, GA) and sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.0 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. All chemicals were analytical reagent used directly and without further purification. Carbon dots (CDs) solid powders were prepared in the laboratory using an electrochemical method based on previous literature [44,45], which can be confirmed by using the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of Fig.S1. Commercial melamine sponge (MS) and Expandable polyethylene (EPE) were purchased from Taobao online website.

Firstly, PVA (0.1 g), SA (0.1 g) and CDs (75 mg) were dispersed in deionized water (10 mL) and magnetically stirred at 90 °C for 1 h to form a homogeneous black solution. A piece of MS (3 cm × 3 cm × 7 cm) was taken to apply the mixed solution uniformly on the top surface and all around the foam (3 cm × 3 cm × 4 cm). To obtain a hydrogel, the coated MS was successively immersed in CaCl2 (2 wt%) solution for 15 min and glutaraldehyde (2 wt%) solution for 30 min, then washed several times with deionized water and dried normally. The above operation was repeated twice to obtain 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel. For comparison, two-dimensional (2D)-SA/PVA-MS (3 cm × 3 cm × 1 cm), 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS (3 cm × 3 cm × 1 cm) and three-dimensional (3D)-SA/PVA-MS (3 cm × 3 cm × 7 cm) hydrogels were prepared following the same procedures except without regulation the of height or without the addition of CDs. MS was processed as 2D-MS (3 cm × 3 cm × 1 cm) and 3D-MS (3 cm × 3 cm × 7 cm).

Solar evaporation experiments were carried out on 2D-MS, 2D-SA/PVA-MS, 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS and 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogels under room temperature of ∼ 20 °C and relative humidity of ∼ 42 % with a solar energy simulator (PLS-SXE300+/300UV, Beijing Perfect Light Company), and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution was applied as simulated seawater. During the experiments, the illuminant was shining vertically on the upper surface of the samples. The corresponding light intensities was tested using a light power meter (CEL-NP2000-2, Beijing China Education AU-LIGHT Co., Ltd.) for each experiment. In order to accurately monitor the water mass, the evaporation device was placed on an electronic precision balance (accuracy of 0.1 mg) to record the water loss mass, and the dark evaporation rate was tested under dark conditions, while the real-time temperature was monitored by an infrared radiation camera (FLIR, A655sc). In order to explore the evaporation rate of the prepared CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator in 2D and 3D devices, the corresponding tests were used. For the 2D solar evaporator (2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS) test: the sample (3 cm × 3 cm × 1 cm) was floated in a beaker with brine, supported by a 3 mm thick (3 cm × 3 cm in the middle blank) in EPE sponge; and for the 3D solar evaporator (3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS) test: the sample (3 cm × 3 cm × 7 cm) was placed in a beaker filled with brine. Water evaporation collection system: the evaporation unit is placed inside a transparent sealed condensation unit with pipes connecting the inlet and outlet and a water storage container. Water evaporation collection system: an integrated evaporation unit was placed inside a transparent sealed condensing unit with pipes connecting the inlet and outlet and a water storage container. The entire system was placed outdoors to utilize sunlight for desalination. For the treatment of dye wastewater and acid and alkali wastewater, these wastewaters are used as a source of water and fresh water is collected in a water evaporation collection system.
In addition, corresponding material characterization, testing instrumentation and calculation of energy efficiency are provided in the Supporting Information .

Fig. 1 a exhibits the schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel by cross-linking the solution prepared from CDs, SA, and PVA on MS, which had excellent water supply ability and photothermal conversion ability. Among them, SA was a natural linear polysaccharide extracted from brown algae, whose carboxyl group could physically cross-link with Ca2+ ions to form a hydrogel [24,46], while SA could form a hybridized hydrogel with PVA, which could enhance its mechanical property by double cross-linking to create a double network structure [30], and could firmly embed photothermal CDs material into the hydrogel network to further improve the evaporation rate (Fig. 1b). The hydrophilic skeleton structure of MS, as a three-dimensional skeleton supporting the hydrogel evaporator, facilitated the transport of water upward by the water transport coating [47]. Next, the surface structure of the as-prepared hydrogel was further observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). In Fig. 1c, the MS presented a periodic large pore structure with several hundred micrometers pore size, exhibiting a smooth surface network feature, while the formed SA/PVA-MS hydrogel adhered tightly to the MS backbone after the cross-linking of the SA/PVA solution on the MS. Compared with SA/PVA-MS hydrogel, the addition of CDs further increased the surface roughness of CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel, forming a stronger and thicker pore wall and effectively preventing pore collapse, which is also reconfirmed through optical pictures (insets of Fig. 1c). Moreover, the black color of CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel was attributed to the homogeneous distribution of CDs in SA/PVA hybrid hydrogel network, which effectively absorbed sunlight and converted it into heat [48,49]. The results obtained from the above observations are such that the resultant CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel possessed a continuously cross-linked reticulated porous structure, which could facilitate the efficient transport of sufficient water from the lateral side of the evaporator to the evaporation surface. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis in Fig.S2 indicated that SA/PVA-MS exhibited a characteristic peak of carbon with low crystallinity around 25°, while with the addition of CDs, CDs/SA/PVA-MS displayed a peak of carbon with high crystallinity, indicating that the hydrogel was successfully doped with CDs [43]. Besides, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra in Fig.S3 revealed a distinct characteristic peak at 1706 cm−1 for CDs, corresponding to the C=O stretching vibration [50–52], which can also be seen in CDs/SA/PVA-MS. And the broad peaks in the range of 3180–3600 cm−1 for all the samples were ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of OH stretching vibration, indicating that the as-synthesized materials exhibited excellent hydrophilicity, which contributed to the improvement of the evaporation rate of the hydrogels.

Conventional physically crosslinked hydrogel suffered from the defect of poor mechanical property, which resulted in the limitation of long-term stable operation of the evaporator [30], in order to overcome this defect, PVA was introduced to form a hybrid hydrogel with double crosslinked network with SA as to enhance its mechanical property. However, SA/PVA hydrogel would cause the uniformity and symmetry of the MS to be damaged, which was not conducive to the transport of water to the upper surface of the evaporator, while the structure of CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel was greatly improved after the addition of CDs, and the hydrogel network could securely fasten the CDs. In Fig. 2 a, the ultra-lightweight property of 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel was exhibited, showing its extremely light weight, meanwhile the adaptability of the hydrogel structure was further proved in Fig. 2b, in which the hydrogel was molded into the word “JUST”, which displayed its flexibility and structural integrity [53]. In the compression test (Fig. 2c), CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel was able to rebound to the initial morphology after compression, displaying excellent compression elasticity, and the stress–strain curves of MS, SA/PVA-MS and CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogels at different compression strains (30 %, 50 % and 70 %) were further specifically analyzed. In Fig. 2d, MS exhibited lower compressive elasticity, on the contrary SA/PVA-MS hydrogel presented stronger load-bearing capacity, which was attributed to the double-layer network structure formed by the double cross-linking of SA and PVA (Fig. 2e). Notably, CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel exhibited excellent linear elastic behavior (Fig. 2f), suggesting that the addition of CDs promotes effective cross-linking and polymerization, which greatly enhanced the load-bearing capacity and overall mechanical properties of the hydrogel. Then after 50-cycle compression fatigue test (at 50 % strain), the stress–strain and force–displacement curves of CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel remained intact and smooth with little fluctuation (Fig. 2g-h), while the maximum stress was increased from 13.7 to 63.9 kPa (nearly 366 % increase) and elastic modulus was increased to 0.022 kPa as compared to that of MS (Fig. 2i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Property of water transfer, anti-salt deposition and structural stability
ce:section.ce:para: In order to ensure the continuous and efficient operation of the solar evaporator, strong water transport capacity, salt deposition resistance and stable structure were carefully considered [54]. It is well known that hydrophilicity serves as one of the important indicators of the water transport capacity of solar evaporators. Based on the contact angle test (Fig. 3 a), all the samples were discovered to possess hydrophilicity, among which 3D-MS needed the longest time (5 s), while 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel could completely absorb the water droplets within 0.5 s, which was attributed to the addition of CDs with hydrophilic groups in the evaporator framework [55,56], effectively enhancing the interaction between water molecules and the evaporator. Then, the water transport capacity of the evaporator was visually inspected with a brown solution (Fig. 3b), in which the surface of 3D-MS was not stained, attributed to the fact that the side of 3D-MS was not capable of water transport. In contrast, the surface of 3D-SA/PVA-MS hydrogel was stained at 30 s with the assistance of SA/PVA hydrogel, while 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel achieved further rapid surface staining with the help of hydrophilic CDs, demonstrating the improved water transport performance of the evaporator. The strong water transfer capability can endow the solar evaporator to achieve excellent salt deposition resistance, which was verified by observing the salt precipitation on the hydrogel surface after evaporation at different salt concentrations (3.5, 7, 15, and 25 wt%) for 5 h. As can be seen in Fig. 3c, no salt particle deposition was observed on the surface of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel. It was due to its three-dimensional water transport structure that water would be rapidly transferred from the bottom to the evaporated surface, while the diffusion effect caused by the difference in salt concentration between the evaporator surface and the simulated seawater would contribute to the transport of the surface salt into the simulated seawater, preventing salt build-up on the evaporation surface., displaying the excellent salt deposition resistance of 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel. In addition, in Fig. 3d, 1 g NaCl was dispersed on the upper surface of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel and dissolved into the simulated seawater after 30 min, due to its porous structure and the introduction of carbon dots that enhance the hydrophilicity and salt solubility of the hydrogel, allowing the salt to dissolve rapidly and redistribute to other areas of the evaporator by capillary action indicating that the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel exhibits effective anti-salt deposition performance, which contributes and ensures its excellent solar evaporation rate. In Fig. 3e, due to the unique hydrophilicity of the composite hydrogel [43], the water absorption rate of 3D-SA/PVA-MS was as high as 5.49 g g−1, and the water absorption rate of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel (water absorption rate: 5.84 g g−1) was further improved attributed to the promotion of CDs to the SA/PVA hydrogel structure stabilization, which promoted the increase of water absorption. The stabilized structure would ensure the continuity of water supply and avoid the deposition of salt particle on the surface of the solar evaporator, necessitating the study of whether the structure of the evaporator is damaged in the wet state. In Fig. 3f, 3D-SA/PVA-MS hydrogel presented a non-uniform and non-asymmetric structure, while 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel displayed a uniform and symmetric structure, which demonstrates that the introduction of CDs can improve the stabilization of the SA/PVA hydrogel structure. In general, swelling change of hydrogels may disrupt the water transport channels of the evaporator, leading to the reduction of water supply, which in turn caused the salt particle deposition on the surface of the evaporator [26]. For the above reasons, the changes in the swelling ratio of the as-prepared samples in different conditioned solutions were recorded. Fig. 3g indicated that the swelling ratio of 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel was stabilized below 12 % under high saline seawater and different pH values, while the swelling behavior of 3D-SA/PVA-MS hydrogel was significantly affected by pH value, which once again proves the immobilization role of CDs in the hydrogel. The cyclic compression tests visualized the excellent water absorption and structural stability of 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel (Fig. 3h), indicating that after 50 compression-release cycles, the structure of 3D-MS was disrupted with severe deformation, while 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel still maintained a stable structure.

The heat transfer process in solar-driven evaporators comprised three primary components: solar energy input, steam energy output and environmental energy loss [57]. The inefficiency of two-dimensional (2D) evaporators was primarily attributed to the partial transfer of heat exchange energy to the surroundings (Fig. 4 a) [58]. Although changing the macrostructure of the evaporator from the traditional 2D structure to the 3D structure can significantly improve the evaporation surface area, effectively utilize the environmental energy, and reduce the heat transfer loss, it is difficult to improve the evaporation rate by simply increasing the exposed area (Fig. 4b). This is because adequate water supply must also be ensured; otherwise, inefficiency may lead to inadequate water transport or severe salt crystallization at the evaporation interface. In this study, the 3D solar evaporator achieved stable and efficient evaporation due to the effective water supply mode of the CDs/SA/PVA hydrogel with MS as a skeleton support. The evaporation efficiency of the solar evaporator was tested using simulated sunlight (Fig. 4c), and the mass loss of water during evaporation under 1 sun illumination was recorded. As can be seen in Fig. 4d, after 60 min of illumination, pure water evaporated slowly (0.16 kg m-2h−1) due to the lack of light-absorbing photothermal materials. In contrast, the evaporation rate of the 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS (3.07 kg m-2h−1) was superior to that of the 2D-SA/PVA-MS (1.36 kg m-2h−1) and 2D-MS (0.96 kg m-2h−1), attributed to the introduction of CDs, which significantly enhanced the photothermal conversion capability of the evaporator surface [43,59–62]. Unfortunately, the conventional 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator failed to avoid downward heat loss. In contrast, the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator increased the evaporation area and utilized the environmental energy to minimize the energy loss during evaporation. For this purpose, we prepared different hydrogel heights (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 cm), and by comparing the evaporation rates of different 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel heights (Fig.S4), we obtained the optimal hydrogel height (4 cm), which corresponded to an evaporation rate of 4.79 kg m-2h−1 and a corresponding energy efficiency of 92.26 % (Fig. 4e). Further measurements confirmed the evaporation rates of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator under side illumination (4.65 kg m-2h−1) and inclined illumination (4.99 kg m-2h−1) (Fig. 4f), which demonstrates that during oblique solar irradiation, both the surface and sides of the evaporator function as heated evaporation zones, efficiently utilizing solar energy from various angles. Notably, even in a 25 wt% NaCl solution, the evaporation rate reached 4.13 kg m-2h−1 (Fig. 4g), attributed to the evaporator's excellent water transport performance. And under 0.5 sun illumination, the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator still achieved an evaporation rate of 2.64 kg m-2h−1 (Fig. 4h). Outstandingly, the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator could be operated stably in high saline water (15 wt%) for a long period of time exceeding 48 h and still maintained a stable evaporation rate (Fig.S5). Beyond saline water and light intensity, the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS was also evaluated for its acid and alkali resistance in water with pH values with 3, 7 and 11 (Fig. 4i), indicating that the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator exhibited stable evaporation performance under varying pH conditions. Moreover, the cyclic performance of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator in Fig. 4j and Fig.S6 reveals that the evaporation rate remained almost unchanged after 48 continuous cycles in saline water (3.5 wt%), indicating its excellent long-term durability for seawater evaporation. In Fig.S7, the differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) plots show the heat flow (W g−1) of three different samples (pure water, SA/PVA-MS and CDs/SA/PVA-MS) under temperature change. The testing results revealed that the heat flow signal of pure water exhibits a sharp decrease after reaching the peak (120 ℃), which corresponds to the evaporation of water, while the peaks of CDs/SA/PVA-MS and SA/PVA-MS samples are wider than that of pure water, and the heat flow signal is gradually weakening, which suggests that the evaporation of water in the sample is slightly different from pure water, because the SA/PVA hydrogel network and CDs could change the evaporation enthalpy of water, leading to the increase of evaporation rate. To highlight the performance advantages of the prepared hydrogel evaporator, the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator was compared with other previously reported hybrid hydrogel evaporators (Fig. 4k) [26,30,63–67], in which finding that the evaporation efficiency of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator surpassed that of most currently available SA/PVA-based composite hydrogel evaporators.

The photothermal conversion efficiency of evaporators was fundamentally determined by their light absorption capacity, thus a UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer was employed to measure the light absorption characteristics of the as-prepared samples. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the absorption rates of SA/PVA-MS hydrogel and MS were relatively lower, but the CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel exhibited a light absorption rate exceeding 83 % within the visible light range primarily due to the addition of photothermal CDs material, which significantly enhanced the light absorption performance. The experimental setup displayed in Fig. 5b was utilized to further evaluate the surface temperature variations of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator under simulated sunlight. Under 1 sunlight intensity irradiation, the surface temperature of the CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator rapidly surged to 61.4 ℃ within 5 min, far surpassing that of pure MS (22.8 ℃) and SA/PVA-MS (26.3 ℃), as depicted in Fig. 5c, corroborating the potent photothermal conversion capability of CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator [68,69]. Notably, the exceptional evaporation performance of CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator was attributed to its excellent thermal management capability, which was further evaluated through the thermal conductivity measurements of the as-prepared samples (Fig. 5d) [70]. The results indicated that all prepared samples exhibited low thermal conductivity, attributable to the inherent insulating pores of the hydrogel. To validate the significance of structure in photothermal materials, the surface roughness of the as-prepared samples was examined using a laser confocal scanning microscope (Fig. 5e). The CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel demonstrated a significant increase in surface roughness compared to SA/PVA-MS hydrogel, owing to the introduction of CDs that formed irregular protrusions on the evaporator surface, which augmented the effective area of the light absorption interface and enhanced surface roughness. Furthermore, to explore why the 3D evaporator exhibited such high evaporation efficiency, an infrared camera was employed to monitor the surface temperature variations of 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS and 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporators at the wet state under simulated evaporation conditions. Within the first hour, the surface temperature of the 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator reached 31.5 ℃ (Fig. 5f1) and the maximum temperature could get up to 33.5 ℃ (Fig. 5g2), in which due to the direct contact of the 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator with water, the overall heat loss was significant, leading to a drop in its temperature. Consequently, transforming the 2D evaporator into 3D evaporator resulted in a rapid rise in the water surface temperature of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator to about 35 ℃ within 20 min (Fig. 5f2), reaching a maximum equilibrium temperature of 38.5 ℃ (Fig. 5g2). The enhanced evaporation system demonstrated greater heat localization ability (ΔT=5 ℃), effectively restricting most of the heat to the evaporation surface, thereby reducing heat loss and promoting steam generation, which indicated that the 3D hydrogel evaporator possesses exceptional thermal management capabilities for facilitating heat localization. The temperature distribution of the evaporator was further evaluated using COMSOL MULTIPHYSICS software. As given in Fig. 5h, compared to the 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator, the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator exhibited higher surface temperatures and stronger localized thermal effects, further validating the stringent thermal management of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator (Fig. 5h1). Conversely, most of the heat in the 2D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator was transmitted downward (Fig. 5h2), which side reflects the outstanding advantage of the 3D evaporator.

As a sustainable technological advancement, solar-powered desalination device leveraged abundant sunlight to extract freshwater from seawater. To validate the feasibility of this technology, a straightforward steam collection system was designed, comprising a solar evaporator, a condenser, and associated storage tanks (Fig. 6 a and b). During the experiment, the evaporation unit was placed on the open rooftop of the experimental building, where the solar radiation intensity and water collection volume (kg m−2) were recorded for the day (May 30, 2024, Zhenjiang, China). As illustrated in Fig. 6c, owing to the high evaporation rate of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator, the condenser changed from transparent to opaque within 10 min of sunlight exposure, a large amount of condensate appeared on the condenser, after 30 min of sunlight irradiation. In Fig. 6d, the evaporation rate gradually increased with the duration of irradiation from 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM. At 13:00 PM, the solar radiation intensity peaked at 0.82 kW m−2, achieving a maximum evaporation rate of 2.8 kg m-2h−1. Additionally, the daily water yield was 14.2 kg m−2, indicating that the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator was capable of producing a substantial amount of freshwater. Obtaining standard-compliant freshwater from saline solutions under various conditions (organic dye solutions, acidic, and alkaline solutions) was also crucial for assessing the evaporator's water purification capability. Therefore, simulated organic dye solution, acidic and alkaline solutions, and the corresponding collected freshwater were measured (Fig. 6e and f). The results indicated that no organic dyes were detected in the collected freshwater, and the pH values turned neutral, proving that the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator displays excellent performance in the removal of organic dyes as well as in the treatment of acidic and alkaline solutions. Furthermore, seawater from the Bohai Sea (coordinate (E120.548, N36.093), in China) was used as the real sea water source, and its ion concentrations were analyzed via inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy. Post-desalination by the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator, the ion concentrations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+) exhibited a significant reduction by 3–4 orders of magnitude compared to natural seawater (Fig. 6g). Notably, the sodium ion concentration in the purified water was only 12.6 mg/L, aligning with the salinity standard for drinking water set by the World Health Organization (WHO) [71,72], which underscored the superior desalination performance of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator.

A 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS hydrogel solar energy interface evaporator with cross-linked fixation by CDs was developed, which exhibited excellent photothermal conversion and ultra-high solar steam evaporation efficiency with achieved a highly solar evaporation rate of up to 4.79 kg m-2h−1. Structural characterizations revealed that the CDs are connected to the double cross-linked network structure of SA/PVA through hydrogen bonding, facilitating effective cross-linking and polymerization, which greatly improved the load-bearing capacity and overall mechanical properties of the hydrogel in MS network. Meanwhile, CDs, as carbon-based materials with excellent light-absorbing ability, was anchored in the structure of the hydrogel and uniformly dispersed in the evaporator, enhancing the photothermal conversion ability of the 3D-CDs/SA/PVA-MS evaporator and achieving ultra-high and stable solar steam generation. Considering the low cost of the used materials and the scalability of the fabrication process, this study provided a reference for further improving the evaporation rate of the SA/PVA-based hydrogel evaporator.
